The responsibility for what is happening in Euro-Turkish relations, and for the growing perplexity of European public opinion towards Turkey, is its, and solely its.

The path to Turkey's accession to the Union passes through Diyarbakir and through the DTP. Otherwise, it will be us, that is, those who have supported the accession process here, who will demand a pause for reflection that risks being definitive.

Yes, thank you, Mr. President. Honourable colleagues, the European Parliament is called upon, as we know, to express itself definitively on food information for consumers.
It must be said immediately that this was an objectively laborious, long, and controversial legislative process that could not be shortened.

It is indeed about harmonizing Community law on food through regulations designed to make information transparent to consumers, avoiding leading them into confused or potentially harmful choices, even to their health.

The report by the Honourable Sommer, which deserves recognition for the effort made to synthesize multiple needs, therefore aims to remedy disputes and different information systems in force in the Union countries.

It is no coincidence that national regulations to this day differ in the denomination of the nature of foodstuffs offered for sale, generating a different system from country to country, also fueling unfair competition to the detriment of potential consumers.
It was thus attempted, on the basis of the European Commission's proposal, to give a new form to the existing legislation, involving on the one hand the food industries and on the other consumers.
Concurrently, the report urges the European food industry to clarify the mandatory information, as well as the labeling and presentation of nutritional values.

In a globalized market, the European Union could not exempt itself from adapting and innovating its legislation on food products, to better protect food trade as well, safeguarding it from an increasingly invasive and fierce international competition.

It is not new that one more often hears about the dangers of food products which, on labels and other forms of nomenclature, display indications that often do not meet food requirements, yet are still marketed as foods and beneficial for human health.

Thank you, Mr. President. The proposal on the rights of passengers traveling by bus aims to improve and protect passengers, and in particular disabled people or those with reduced mobility.
This is the first regulation on bus passenger rights and it should guarantee passengers, as is the case in the rail and air sectors.
The position in the second reading aims to establish a series of rights for passengers who decide to travel by bus.

I agree with rapporteur Cancian's line, whom I thank for the excellent work done, and I believe that the scope of application of the regulation should be modified, excluding regional services when they are integrated with urban and suburban services.
It would be right and proper to guarantee the rights of passengers in case of accidents, cancellations, or delays at departure, as had already been decided in the first reading by Parliament.

Equally important is the issue of information on passenger rights. It would indeed be useful to provide passengers with information on connections with other modes of transport. This will also ensure dialogue between passenger transport services via bus and those via rail.

As for disabled people and people with reduced mobility, it is necessary to guarantee maximum protection. I would propose a deadline of 24 hours instead of 48, in order to request assistance, as well as greater guarantees for compensation and the replacement of devices that ensure the mobility of differently-abled individuals, in case of loss or damage.
Furthermore, taking into account the needs of passengers with reduced mobility, any architectural barrier should be eliminated and existing infrastructure improved to make them accessible.
This regulation aims to enhance the competitiveness of the bus sector, foster intermodality, and most importantly, improve passenger travel conditions.

Thank you, Mr. President. To date, the recommendations adopted by the General Fisheries Commission for the Mediterranean have been transposed into Community law on a provisional basis through the annual regulations on fishing opportunities.
These concern measures for the conservation, management, exploitation, control, and commercialization of fishery and aquaculture products in the Mediterranean.
The permanent nature of such recommendations, however, requires a more stable legal instrument for their incorporation into community law.
It is therefore appropriate to incorporate the recommendations in question through a single legislative act, to which future recommendations can be added.
Today we did this, and this will improve legal certainty, while at the same time constituting an important step towards simplification.
At this point, the contracting parties of the CGMPM have the obligation to ensure full implementation of the agreed measures, effective from the established date.
A timely transposition was therefore necessary in order to make such international measures directly applicable to natural or legal persons at community level, and to ensure legal certainty.

More than 20 years have passed since the signing of the Schengen agreements.
No one questions the advantages of free movement, but it must also be recognized that the current arrangement is no longer adequate for current and future needs.

For this reason, the Member States and the entire EU must commit to reviewing Schengen's operating mechanisms, so that the freedom of movement does not come at the expense of the legitimate need for security on the part of our citizens.

As far as we know so far, the Commission's guidelines can also be partly shared, especially with regard to the introduction of a mechanism that allows states to temporarily reinstate border controls in case of emergency.
However, we need to see how this will come about, and if declarations will be followed by actions, especially concrete results.

Certainly, it will also be necessary to undertake a general reform of the European border protection system, especially the external ones, on which Frontex must quickly become an agency with real powers of intervention and support for national authorities.
We must protect our external borders if we want to continue with this Schengen.
It is time for courageous choices in this Europe, if we do not want to merely survive.

The crisis has profoundly marked the real lives of European citizens and businesses, and risks, if we do not react, irreversibly lowering social and cultural quality, the well-being, the future of young people and of Europeans.

against the recession. Remedies against an unbalanced development model that has placed people one step below profit. Remedies against resurgent nationalism, which indeed prevents Europe from making the choices that are incumbent upon it today.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, Mr. Representative of the Council, and Madam Commissioner, first of all, I too would like to welcome positively, as other colleagues have done, the anti-corruption package that the European Commission approved on June 6th.
Corruption is a transnational phenomenon, and to combat it, measures on a European scale are necessary.
The vast majority of our European citizens, according to Eurobarometer estimates, believes that Europe should do more.
How can one blame them?

In many member states, the political will to combat corruption has been lacking. One only needs to consider that there are still European countries that have not signed the United Nations Convention against Corruption.

If we think about the devastating impact of corruption on democratic processes, on citizens' rights, and on trust in the rule of law, it is clear that the national countermeasures adopted by the member states to date are insufficient to contain a growing phenomenon.

It is the European Union that must give a unified and strong response, especially now, in the midst of an unprecedented economic crisis, which is weighing on people's lives, on the sustainability of social security and welfare systems, on the capacity of institutions to guarantee essential services.
Annually, corruption causes the loss of 120 billion euros, amounting to 1% of the European Union's GDP. And everyday, it siphons off resources from the Union and member states that
and could be used to provide fundamental services—education, childhood, employment, transport—in short, to divert clean resources from supporting people.

One must therefore have more courage and more steadfastness, both by monitoring the implementation of anti-corruption measures and by intensifying and tightening the measures to combat corruption at the Union level.

In particular, we cannot speak of fighting corruption if we are not able to adopt at least minimum standards for the definition of the crime of corruption, of which there is no trace in the package just launched.
The same applies to the definition of uniform parameters for measuring the magnitude of the phenomenon in member states, essential for devising an effective response.
We are in favor, for example, of relaunching the network of EU contact points against corruption, as proposed by the resolution that we will vote on tomorrow.
The crisis is not new; it has already existed for some years.
Perhaps the time has come to make a critical assessment of the policies followed so far. Has everything always been done well?
We have always conducted appropriate analyses of the situation.

The commission must go beyond its weakness. Let it be said that the priority is: fighting unemployment and creating jobs, redistribution of wealth, and a strategy of public investments, also changing the rules of the new stability pact.

Honourable colleagues, Finland and the Netherlands objected in the Council for a very simple reason. Bulgaria and Romania are not able today to guarantee effective border control, and are the scene of widespread corruption involving the political class and organized crime.
Thinking this way, it's not the populist parties or movements, but Europol in its 2010 report on organized crime.
There is no populist propaganda, but a serious security problem.

Should Bulgaria enter the Schengen area at this moment, police and security agencies foresee that the Bulgarian borders, especially the maritime ones, would become an easy target for Turkish crime and that of Eastern Europe. And Bulgaria and Romania would become the crossroads for human trafficking and drug trafficking.

It is natural that some Member States have many reservations about the real capacity of Bulgaria and Romania to guarantee effective border controls against organized crime and corruption. And it is therefore absolutely natural that they have opposed their entry into Schengen.
To allow it now means paving the way for transnational crime and opening a breach in European borders precisely where there is a very strong migratory pressure from Turkey.
The reasons that arise are numerous, and Europe cannot ignore them.
I therefore ask what the intentions of the Commission and the Council are regarding this delicate issue, which in my opinion has been handled so far with
adequacy and without taking into account the dangers that the entry of Bulgaria and Romania would entail, according to the competent bodies in matters of security and international crime.

In my opinion, the Commission and the Council should postpone the entry of these two countries into Schengen and give the go-ahead only when there will be reasonable certainty that the entry of Bulgaria and Romania will not have repercussions on the security of European citizens.

The phosphates present in domestic washing machine detergents end up in water systems, in rivers, streams, and European lakes.

Phosphorus causes an acceleration of the reproduction of various forms of life. Consequently, algae and other fast-growing plant species proliferate excessively when there is an increase in the presence of phosphorus in an aquatic environment.
This excessive growth of algae removes oxygen from other living organisms, can also produce toxins, destroying fish and plant species, thereby reducing the quality of the water itself.

I therefore expressed my favorable vote for the text voted on today, because to purify European water resources, it is desirable to reduce the amount of phosphorus that is discharged into the waters of our continent.

Mr. Commissioner. Today, Europe is asked to undertake a new task of coordinating industrial policies, and now even the most severe supporters of the autonomy of private initiative are calling for the exercise of this role.
Without a greater Europeanization of national strategies, even from the perspective of employment protection, the current crisis will not be overcome, and there will be no recovery.
The automotive crisis is not just production overcapacity; it's a dramatic loss of jobs.
But the crisis now seems structural and concerns the very fate of the car, of the product, as it is today.
We want to keep the automotive industry in Europe, and we don't want to lose the existing skills and know-how, the professional skills.
We therefore ask that a major restructuring and relaunch project be launched, and that this be done quickly, because costs are proportional to the time.
The restructuring cannot be solely the result of fragmented national decisions or of individual companies.
It is necessary to at least try to coordinate and guide this painful process at community level, bringing the interested parties to the table, with social dialogue.

I know it will be an arduous task, because the national interests of individual companies are not aligned.

However, since the competitive struggle underway in the European Union is very ambiguous and sometimes risks being unfair, I believe that ultimately it is in the interest of all countries to have an effective community impetus and guidance to jointly confront the increasingly aggressive global market.

Yes, thank you, President. As we recalled in this chamber, next December, the United Nations Agency dedicated to communication and information technology will meet in Dubai for the World Conference on International Communications.
The subject is the renegotiation of international regulations on telecommunications.

We cannot forget—I am pleased with the words of Commissioner Tajani—the work that has been done up until today, which cannot be called into question, nor can we allow the authority of the European Union to be bypassed by

a supranational structure like the ITU, of which hundreds of states from all over the world are members, and many of which are known for a problem related to democracy, transparency, and freedom of expression.
Therefore, it is not incorrect to believe that the pillars of the internet are in danger, namely freedom, participation, openness, and innovation.

Therefore, I believe it is necessary to protect the role of the internet, and the role that the internet, especially, can play in relaunching economic growth, but not only that, but also to guarantee opportunities for all.
I therefore hope that the Commission works with the objective that Europe in Dubai is united and speaks with one voice.

Many controversial proposals have been presented at the negotiating table of the World Conference on International Telecommunications. In particular, those that propose changes to internet governance, which question the neutrality of the network, with negative repercussions for consumers.
Internet freedom is therefore in danger, or more precisely, freedom of expression and data protection.
What some states have proposed would in fact allow a government to stick its nose not only in the communications of its citizens, but even in the data that transits on their own networks.

The step from here to the limitation of freedom of expression and to censorship is extremely short, and therefore I believe it is in contradiction with the fundamental principles of the European Union.

In recent years, the extraction of hydrocarbons, the so-called unconventional ones, including shale gas and oil, has led to an unprecedented transformation in global energy markets.
Europe, to face such a situation and to be less dependent on hydrocarbon imports, will therefore have to rely on its own potential gas and shale reserves.
In this regard, some Member States have already authorized the search for shale gas and are preparing for the extraction phase, should the discovery allow it.
In such a context, it becomes necessary to monitor the regulations related to shale gas extraction within the Union's borders so that the effects of its

extraction, undoubtedly valid from an economic perspective, do not create environmental problems due to the possible release of harmful substances into the aquifers and into the atmosphere. Thank you.

But I wanted to focus on artisanal fishing, on the Ferreira report, which I welcome and for which I offer my congratulations.

I wanted to say something: with the previous Common Fisheries Policy, it has only contributed to widening the gap between the institutions that regulate fishing and its true actors, namely the fishermen.

Unfortunately, until now in Europe, the lack of a true and proper definition of artisanal fishing has penalized those who have always understood fishing as an activity linked to the traditions of their own place, their own seas, and their own cuisine.

And so the conviction grew that the approach taken by the European Commission was to standardize all fishing in Europe under large-scale industrial fishing, following solely market principles and not listening to the reasons of thousands of fishermen who for a couple of

For decades, they have operated according to rules that are pertinent to the conservation of the ecosystem, on which I can only agree, but which have never preserved the working activities of the micro-economy of artisanal fishing.
This own-initiative report is a strong signal to the Commission and one that can translate into practical actions to the benefit of the fishing micro-economy.
The report, in fact, highlights a series of advantages, the allocation of a minimum sum to small-scale fishing from the European Fund.

It will be necessary to encourage projects with integrated solutions that benefit coastal communities as a whole, and furthermore, it will be essential, Commissioner, to encourage young people's access.

Thank you. Thank you, Mr. President. Finning is an unacceptable practice from an environmental point of view, as it threatens the sustainability of some shark species which, from
social and economic and because it implies the waste of a food item with commercial value, as well as from an ethical point of view, because it demonstrates a total lack of respect for animal life.

Although this practice has been forbidden since 2003, in European waters and for the European fleet, the Commission has decided to present a proposal that aims to also eliminate the derogation from the ban on shark finning on board Union fishing vessels.

Such a proposal, in addition to further guaranteeing sharks the protection they need, will thus allow the European Union to actively contribute to the global effort underway to eradicate the cruel practice of shark finning also in other parts of the world.
